text: The patient is positioned prone as the TAM kit is installed. The scope is centralized on the residual cancer tissue, which is visible between the five and ten o'clock positions.

The surgeon begins dissection with cautery from the lowest portion of the rectal wall, moving upwards to both the left and right sides into the perirectal fatty tissue, carefully avoiding contact with the tumor. A full thickness resection is performed to ensure adequate safety margins.

The surgeon encounters poor tissue quality with noticeable edema and focal fibrosis due to prior radiotherapy. The anterior rectal wall is opened above the peritoneal reflection, revealing adjacent bowel loops. Dissection proceeds cautiously to prevent damage to surrounding intra-abdominal organs.

The surgical team experiences a brief loss of pneumorectum due to perforation, causing the intra-abdominal pressure to equalize and resulting in less optimal exposure of the operation area. This necessitates a more extended, 360-degree resection. The sudden pneumoperitoneum affects the patient's ventilation capacity, which is managed despite his COPD.

The surgeon completes the resection by dividing the remaining strands of tissue, fully excising the tumor. The excision cavity is inspected for hemostasis, and the peritoneal defect is examined to confirm the absence of intra-abdominal trauma.

The surgeon identifies the lumen of the proximal rectum and closes the peritoneal defect using a continuous VLOG-TRIO suture, ensuring no adjacent bowel is incorporated. The rectum is then thoroughly rinsed to minimize tumor seeding and reduce bacterial load.

The surgeon uses multiple V-lock trio sutures to approximate the mucosa, achieving closure and hemostatic control of the 360-degree defect. Careful attention is given to the suturing technique to prevent twisting and stenosis, which would be difficult to correct later.
